title: Florentines
subtitle: Caramelised nut and fruit wafers with a dark chocolate back
excerpt: Twenty florentines: a mixture of double cream, sugar, and butter cooked to a soft caramel, stirred through with flaked almonds, glacé cherries, and dried cranberries, dropped onto trays in small mounds, baked until lacy and golden, then turned over and coated on the flat side with dark chocolate.
sourceNotes: Florentines are documented in European confectionery from the nineteenth century as a caramelised nut wafer originating in the French and Austrian patisserie tradition. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the almond wafer section documents a thin, caramelised almond biscuit made from a cooked sugar and cream mixture spread thin on a baking tray and finished with a chocolate coating. The recipe here follows the public-domain method of a soft caramel base with flaked almonds and dried fruit, finished with chocolate.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Florentines keep in an airtight tin for up to 2 weeks. The chocolate sets harder in a cool kitchen — store at room temperature or they soften.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; watch closely from 8 minutes — the caramel darkens fast

recipeTools.notes: For lifting warm florentines off the paper and coating with chocolate.

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic appearance of a florentine as it bakes: the caramel mixture spreads outward and the sugar between the nut and fruit pieces bubbles and sets in a thin, open lacework pattern. The lacy structure is structural — the caramel fills the spaces and holds the disc together when set. A florentine that has not spread enough is too thick and chewy; one that has spread too much will have thin edges that burn before the centre is caramelised.

content.text: Florentines are concentrated: fruit and nuts bound in caramel, no flour, no egg. The
of the mixture in the oven is what creates the characteristic thin, open disc. They require close attention during baking: the line between caramelised and burned is about 90 seconds at 180°C fan.

attrs.title: Reshape immediately out of the oven
attrs.body: Florentines spread unevenly in the oven. As soon as they come out, use a round metal cutter or the back of a spoon to push the ragged edges inward and reshape each into a neat circle while the caramel is still liquid. They set within 2 minutes of coming out.

items.groupLabel: For the fruit and nut mix
For the fruit and nut mix
For the fruit and nut mix

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Combine the {{double-cream}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{honey}} in a small saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil and cook for 1 minute, stirring, until the mixture thickens slightly. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{plain-flour}}, {{flaked-almonds}}, {{glace-cherries}}, and {{dried-cranberries}}. The mixture will be loose and sticky.

Drop heaped teaspoons of the mixture onto the lined trays, spacing them at least 6 cm apart — they spread considerably. Flatten each mound slightly. Bake for 9–11 minutes until the edges are deep golden brown and the centre is caramelised. Watch from 8 minutes. While the florentines are still hot and liquid, use a round cutter to push the edges inward and neaten each disc into a circle. Leave to cool and set on the trays (about 5 minutes) then transfer to a rack.

Melt the {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring until smooth. Turn each florentine caramel-side down. Spread a thin, even layer of chocolate over the flat side with a palette knife. Leave chocolate-side up to set on baking paper — the chocolate will firm at room temperature in about 20–30 minutes, or faster in the fridge. Once set, store layered with baking paper in an airtight tin.
